did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (specifically a fine-tuned EfficientNet-B5 model) and Topological Data Analysis (TDA) with persistence homology to extract features from dermoscopic images for the diagnosis of basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The hybrid TDA-DL model achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.4% and an AUC of 0.995.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a hybrid Topological Data Analysis and Deep Learning (TDA-DL) model for Basal Cell Carcinoma diagnosis. It details specific methodologies (fine-tuning EfficientNet-B5, persistence homology), datasets used (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, NIH), and reports new quantitative results (97.4% accuracy, 0.995 AUC) on a holdout test set, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hybrid Topological Data Analysis and Deep Learning for Basal Cell Carcinoma Diagnosis
journal: Journal of Imaging Informatics in Medicine
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid Topological Data Analysis and Deep Learning (TDA-DL) model to diagnose basal cell carcinoma (BCC) using dermoscopic images. The methodology combined persistence homology features extracted from segmented telangiectasia and skin lesions with deep learning features generated by fine-tuning a pre-trained EfficientNet-B5 model. The dataset comprised 1,000 BCC images and 1,000 non-BCC images derived from the HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH datasets. Evaluation on a holdout test set demonstrated that the hybrid model achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.4% and an AUC of 0.995. The results indicate that incorporating telangiectasia features via TDA significantly improves BCC diagnosis compared to deep learning models alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The study used 1,000 BCC images derived from HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH datasets.
One of the non-BCC classes used in the binary classification task. Included 400 images in the non-BCC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. non-BCC
all_task_types.details: The study performs a binary classification task to diagnose BCC using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC or non-BCC) using a hybrid TDA-DL approach with majority voting on class probabilities. The primary evaluation metrics are accuracy (97.4%), sensitivity, specificity, and precision on a holdout test set of 395 images.

datasets_used.name: Custom BCC vs Non-BCC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of 2,000 dermoscopic skin lesion images (1,000 BCC and 1,000 non-BCC) derived from three public sources: HAM10000 (ISIC 2018), ISIC 2019, and NIH studies (CA153927-01 and CA101639-02A2). The images were resized to 448x448 for processing. The dataset is publicly available via Zenodo.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net model for telangiectasia segmentation and to fine-tune the EfficientNet-B5 model for feature extraction.
use.validation: Used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning during the fine-tuning of EfficientNet-B5.
use.testing: Used as a holdout test set of 395 images to evaluate the final hybrid TDA-DL model's performance (accuracy, AUC, etc.).
use.other: The telangiectasia masks and corresponding images are openly shared for benchmarking purposes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC 2018)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. Used as a source for the custom dataset.

per_class.Non-BCC: Included in the 1000 non-BCC images used from this dataset

use.training: Source of 127 BCC images and a portion of the 1,000 non-BCC images for the custom dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public skin lesion dermoscopy dataset. Used as a source for the custom dataset.

per_class.Non-BCC: Included in the 1000 non-BCC images used from this dataset

use.training: Source of 90 BCC images and a portion of the 1,000 non-BCC images for the custom dataset.

datasets_used.name: NIH Studies (CA153927-01 and CA101639-02A2)
datasets_used.description: Datasets from NIH studies used to provide BCC images for the custom dataset.

use.training: Source of 783 BCC images for the custom dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract and summarize topological features (birth/death pairs) from persistence diagrams for classification.
To calculate the central tendency of midlife coordinates and normalized lifespan distributions.
To measure the dispersion of midlife coordinates and normalized lifespan distributions.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, AUC, Jaccard Score
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper explicitly states that it does not incorporate tests for statistical significance, noting that choosing suitable hypothesis testing methods in the context of deep learning poses challenges and requires significant resources. The study prioritized predictive accuracy and model generalization over group mean comparisons.

Custom BCC vs Non-BCC Dataset (Holdout T.description: A holdout test set of 395 skin lesion images (195 BCC and 200 non-BCC) derived from HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and NIH datasets.

U-Net Telangiectasia Segmentation.metric: Non-blank mask proportion for non-BCC classes

Custom BCC vs Non-BCC Dataset (Holdout T.notes: The hybrid TDA-DL model with vessels achieved the highest accuracy (0.974) and AUC (0.995) on the holdout test set. The paper notes that the final sensitivity was slightly lesser than Serrano et al. [21] but achieved higher accuracy and precision overall. No statistical significance tests were performed.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Topological Data Analysis and Deep Learning (TDA-DL) model combining EfficientNet-B5 and Random Forest
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet-B5 was pre-trained on ImageNet (implied by 'pre-trained' and standard EfficientNet usage) and fine-tuned on the custom BCC vs non-BCC dataset.

deep_learning_component.backbone_structure: 14 stages. Stage 1: Conv 3x3 + BN + Swish. Stages 2-8: MBConv blocks (kernel sizes 3x3, 5x5, 3x3). Stage 9: Conv 1x1 with 2048 filters. Stages 10-11: Batch Normalization and Softmax activation. Stage 12: Global Average Pooling. Stages 13-14: Dropout and Dense layer.

topology_component.feature_extraction: Persistence Statistics (PS) extracted from persistence diagrams of segmented telangiectasia and skin lesions.

ensemble_component.fusion_method: Majority voting on class probabilities from two separate Random Forest classifiers (one for DL features, one for TDA features)

training_details.hardware: Single 32 GB Nvidia V100 graphics card

classification_or_output_head.output_mechanism: Majority voting of probabilities from DL-based Random Forest and TDA-based Random Forest

preprocessing_techniques: Square cropping of images, centering on the lesion area
Histogram stretching for U-Net input images
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for U-Net input images
Brightness enhancement for U-Net input images
Dilation of ground truth vessel masks with a 3 × 3 structuring element
Closing of ground truth vessel masks with a 2 × 2 structuring element

Geometric data augmentation for U-Net training: rotation (+30° to -30° in reflect mode), horizontal and vertical flip, width shift (-0.2 to +0.2), height shift (-0.2 to +0.2), and shear (-0.2 to +0.2)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during U-Net training. Techniques included: rotation (+30° to -30° in reflect mode to preserve vessel continuity), horizontal and vertical flip, width shift (range -0.2 to +0.2), height shift (range -0.2 to +0.2), and shear (range -0.2 to +0.2).

conclusions: The hybrid Topological Data Analysis and Deep Learning (TDA-DL) model achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.4% and an AUC of 0.995 on a holdout test set of 395 skin lesions for BCC diagnosis.

Incorporating telangiectasia features via TDA significantly improves BCC diagnosis compared to deep learning models alone, as evidenced by performance jumps in accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and precision when vessel features were added.
The hybrid TDA-DL model outperformed standalone deep learning models (EfficientNet-B5, EfficientNet-B0, InceptionV3) and TDA-only models (Random Forest with persistence statistics).

The study demonstrates that telangiectasia features improve BCC diagnosis and that TDA techniques hold the potential to improve deep learning performance by providing a clinically explainable aspect of the diagnosis.
The final sensitivity of the proposed model was slightly lower than that of Serrano et al. [21], but it achieved higher accuracy and precision overall.

The study acknowledges limitations including the lack of statistical significance testing due to the challenges and resource requirements of choosing suitable hypothesis testing methods in deep learning contexts.
The study acknowledges that the datasets used lack diversity in skin color, which restricts the generalizability and applicability of the model.
The absence of a common dataset with established reference data complicates the creation of a reliable benchmark for comparison with other studies.
The ground truth vessel mask marking was supervised by a single dermatologist, which is a limitation regarding annotation consistency.
Future research directions include incorporating a broader range of clinical features and applying statistical techniques such as ANOVA with post hoc tests to address significant differences.
important_remarks: The study lacks statistical significance testing, citing challenges and resource requirements for choosing suitable hypothesis testing methods in deep learning contexts.
The datasets used (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, NIH) lack diversity in skin color, which restricts the generalizability and applicability of the model to darker skin tones.

Ground truth vessel mask annotation was supervised by a single dermatologist (WVS) with only one team observer annotating each mask, posing a limitation regarding annotation consistency and inter-observer variability.
The absence of a common dataset with established reference data complicates the creation of a reliable benchmark for comparison with other studies.
The final sensitivity of the proposed hybrid model (0.972) was slightly lower than that of Serrano et al. [21] (0.993), although it achieved higher accuracy and precision.

The model was trained on a curated dataset of 2,000 images (1,000 BCC, 1,000 non-BCC) derived from public sources, rather than using the full public datasets directly, which may introduce selection bias.
The study acknowledges that raising sensitivity and specificity remains an ongoing challenge in skin cancer diagnosis automation.
